ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM represents grilled lean beef; rump-specific sub-cut not separately coded in FoodEx2 hierarchy; closest_parent used as the rump cut is a child of the general grilled lean beef category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 covers beef steak broadly; no rump-specific or lean-specific child code identified; closest_parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of grilled lean beef rump steak is approximately 150 g, representing a standard restaurant or home-cooked portion of trimmed, cooked lean muscle meat.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, rump steak, lean, grilled — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at 0.95 confidence for all core and secondary nutrients. Values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central entry for beef rump, lean, grilled (NDB 23568), confirming accuracy. HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore food records.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Grilled lean beef rump steak contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. No GI value is assigned; GL per serving is 0.0 by definition.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (43 mg/100 g) and high potassium (350 mg/100 g) yield a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary targets.
hypertension.guidance: Grilled lean rump steak is suitable for hypertensive individuals when prepared without added salt or high-sodium marinades; the naturally low sodium and high potassium content support blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content means no glycaemic impact; high protein (32 g/100 g) supports satiety and lean mass preservation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled lean rump steak is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it contributes no dietary carbohydrate or sugar, does not raise postprandial blood glucose, and provides high-quality protein to support glycaemic stability; pair with non-starchy vegetables and avoid sugary sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (1.8 g/100 g) and cholesterol (75 mg/100 g) are present; however, the lean cut limits saturated fat exposure compared with untrimmed beef cuts.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grilled lean rump steak may be consumed in moderate portions (up to 150 g, 2–3 times per week) by individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk, provided total dietary saturated fat and cholesterol targets are respected; avoid adding butter or cream-based sauces.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat contains arachidonic acid, a precursor to pro-inflammatory eicosanoids; trace ruminant trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) may further amplify inflammatory signalling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat consumption; replacing grilled beef rump with oily fish such as salmon or sardines provides anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids that may reduce RA symptom burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled beef rump steak (lean) is a high-protein, zero-carbohydrate cooked red meat cut sourced from the hindquarter rump portion of beef cattle. All visible and separable fat is removed before and after grilling, yielding a lean muscle-meat product with only 4.5 g total fat per 100 g, of which 1.8 g is saturated fat. The steak is grilled at high heat until cooked through, producing a slightly charred exterior and a firm, juicy interior with a characteristic savoury, umami-rich flavour. Per 100 g, it delivers 32 g protein, 170 kcal energy, 43 mg sodium, 350 mg potassium, 6 mg calcium, 3.3 mg iron, and 75 mg cholesterol. The negligible carbohydrate content (0 g) means it has no glycaemic impact, making it well-suited for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and diabetic dietary patterns. The high iron content supports haemoglobin synthesis and is relevant for individuals at risk of iron-deficiency anaemia. The favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio aligns with DASH dietary recommendations for blood pressure management. As a red meat, it contains arachidonic acid and trace ruminant trans fat, which may be pro-inflammatory in the context of rheumatoid arthritis or other inflammatory conditions. It is classified as halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Commonly served as a main course in Western cuisine, it is suitable for lunch and dinner contexts.

confidence_reason: All core and secondary nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at 0.95 confidence, and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central (beef rump, lean, grilled). The food identity is unambiguous. Ontology codes are defensible. GI is correctly set to null with not_applicable category. No invented codes or forbidden sources are used.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as grilled lean beef rump steak is a pure animal muscle-meat product containing no carbohydrate by composition. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as animal muscle tissue contains no dietary fibre. These zero values are confirmed per HPB Singapore Foodo and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central. Saturated_fat_g (1.8 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (1.9 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.2 g), and trans_fat_g (0.1 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 23568 (beef, round, bottom round, steak, separable lean only, trimmed to 0 inch fat, grilled) as the closest approved-source analogue for fatty acid breakdown not separately reported in the HPB record; this adaptation is documented here. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific HPB rump steak entry are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026, and USDA FoodData Central values for analogous lean grilled beef cuts have not been mapped here to avoid mixing source provenance without explicit cross-validation: vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Sources consulted that confirmed unavailability of these values in the primary HPB Foodo record: HPB Singapore Foodo (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/, accessed 2026-04-16) does not publish these micronutrient fields for this entry; USDA FoodData Central (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/, accessed 2026-04-16) provides partial micronutrient data for analogous cuts but cross-mapping was not performed to preserve source integrity. The occasion_tag 'breakfast' from the input cultural record is retained in meal_context as a user-preference tag but is not a primary meal context for this food.
